repo_prior_art.cite: none; closest catalogue entry is 3.1 Manski bounded-outcome bounds, which has no spell-history metric-extension kernel

published_prior_art.cite: McShane1934 and Whitney1934: classical Lipschitz extension envelopes already supply the scalar extension algebra

repo_prior_art.cite: closest are pid_dynamic_iv_compliance_v2, pid_long_term_surrogate_v1, and pid_did_anticipation_bounded_v1; none states reversible spell-history metric sharpness

published_prior_art.cite: ChernozhukovFernandezValHahnNewey2013 and JunLeeShin2016 use time-homogeneity/stationarity; DiemerShiSwanson2024 gives assumption-free sustained-strategy bounds, not finite-rho metric faces

repo_prior_art.cite: none; conditional long-run wrapper around Conjecture 1

published_prior_art.cite: Han2021 studies finite dynamic treatment effects but not this long-run metric-extension partial-ID interval; the limit step is routine once weights are fixed

structure_flags.label: Bibliography
structure_flags.one_line: The cited JBES comparator is Jun, Lee, and Shin (2016), but the body/bibkey/bibliography list it as Lee and Shin and omit Jun; pages are also inconsistent with the verified 302--311 record.

novelty_flags.label: Proposal tier
novelty_flags.one_line: tier=field below novelty_target=flagship; to reach flagship the draft must identify a named published open problem or strict-extension target, and compare against generic finite discrete sharp-bounds engines such as DuarteFinkelsteinKnoxMummoloShpitser2023 rather than only stationarity/g-formula comparators.

soundness_flags.label: Theorem 1
soundness_flags.one_line: The theorem lacks an observed-anchor feasibility assumption: if anchored means violate the rho-Lipschitz inequalities within a current-treatment component, the feasible scalar-map set is empty and the stated sharp interval is not well-posed.

soundness_flags.label: Conjecture 2
soundness_flags.one_line: The locked target allows arbitrary weights w_{t,T}, but the long-run result calls the limit Cesaro and claims eventual periodic/stationary anchor arrays imply convergence; that reduction is only valid after pinning Cesaro or another regular summability scheme.

strengthening_paths.suggestion: Add the anchor-compatibility condition |a_t(s)-a_t(s')| <= rho d_sp(s,s') for all observed same-arm anchors, and state that otherwise the identified set is empty.
Pin the long-run weights to uniform Cesaro weights, or state precise regularity conditions on triangular-array weights under which eventual periodic/stationary support-value arrays imply convergence.

Lift the tier by adding a strict-improvement theorem: exhibit an open set of observable reversible-spell laws where finite rho gives a strictly tighter interval than Diemer-Shi-Swanson/Manski unrestricted bounds, with endpoint laws saturating both faces.

Add a comparison to generic discrete causal-bounds/LP machinery and explain exactly why the McShane-Whitney face formula is an analytic sharpness certificate rather than a relabelled finite-support optimization.

recommended_next_step: Revise the feasibility and long-run statements, fix the Jun-Lee-Shin citation, and strengthen the literature/open-problem positioning enough to justify the requested flagship tier.
